EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system across Southern New Mexico/Northern Mexico began opening up and moved into the Texas Hill Country late on the 2nd and during the early morning hours of the 3rd. A strong upper level jet streak rounded the base of this trough from old Mexico into Southern Texas and into the Lower Mississippi Valley early on the 3rd as well. At the surface, a low pressure system formed off the Southeast Texas coast and moved North Northeast from Southeast Texas into Northwest Louisiana with a returning warm front from the coastal parishes of Louisiana. Heavy rainfall developed along and north of the Interstate 30 corridor of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas while strong to severe thunderstorms developed across portions of East Central Texas and Northern Louisiana along and in the wake of the returning warm front. The result was wind damage from these thunderstorms in addition to some flash flooding north and west of the Interstate 30 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerline was downed at the intersection of Hwy. 71 and Hwy. 9 in Campti, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold arctic airmass filtered into the region in the preceding days of January 11th with temperatures mostly in the lower 30s. An overrunning moist airmass resulted in the development of mostly light rain across Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas, the Northern third of Northeast Texas as well as Claiborne and Union Parishes in North Central Louisiana. After midnight on the 11th, this precipitation became light freezing rain but due to the temperature being at or just below freezing, ice accumulation was relegated to trees and elevated exposed objects including powerlines and some bridge surfaces. Ice accumulations were mostly near one tenth of an inch across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of slow moving thunderstorms stretched from Southeast Texas and across Calcasieu Parish. Heavy rain from the storms fell on an area with already full ditches and saturated ground. This produced street flooding in and around Lake Charles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets and intersections were flooded in and around Lake Charles and slow moving storms produced 2 to 4 inches over the city. Several roads became nearly impassable including Tank Farm, College, Prien Lake, and Luke Powers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent stationary front provided the focus for numerous thunderstorms across southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of the 15th and early morning of the 16th. A wake low moved across the northern Gulf of Mexico behind the thunderstorm complex, producing several reports of strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from nickel to golfball size was reported from the Garyville, Reserve, and Laplace communities as a severe thunderstorm moved across the parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall was widespread across Northern Louisiana during the morning hours of May 18th.  This convection developed from an upper level disturbance that moved into the region from Central Texas.  Moisture was plentiful across the region as the upper level flow was from the southwest.  This allowed the disturbance to tap moisture from not only the Eastern Pacific but also the Gulf of Mexico.  These storms trained across the same general area resulting in some impressive rainfall totals.  The end result was widespread flooding across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported in streets and homes off of Keithville-Kingston Road and Domino Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent southwest flow aloft dominated the Southern Plains and Lower Mississippi Valley. Shortwave troughs embedded in this flow influenced the Four State Region during the late morning, afternoon and evening hours of May 24th. Daytime heating resulted in the instability needed to produce showers and thunderstorms. Some of these storms produced strong and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Sportsman Lodge Road in Campti, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced several severe thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana during the early morning hours of the 26th, including at least one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the east bank of the Mississippi River, damaging a pier at the foot of Williams Boulevard and snapping hardwood trees. The highest EF-1 damage occurred at the start of the tornado track from the Mississippi River to along Reverend Richard Wilson Drive with structural damage reported to homes and a business. The tornado continued to track northwest to Kenner Avenue, where it caused part of a roof to peel back on a single family home. The tornado then weakened to EF-0 as it lifted on the north side of the railroad tracks. Estimated peak wind was 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across South Louisiana during the afternoon of the 9th with a couple of storms pulsing to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst over the Lake Charles Regional Airport produced a wind gust of 74 MPH. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across South Louisiana during the afternoon of the 9th with a couple of storms pulsing to severe limits.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch in diameter hail was reported in Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough stalled over the Southwest States for multiple days while several short waves rotated around the base of the larger trough. This produced periods of severe storms and flooding in Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager of Calcasieu Parish reported roof damage to The Racquet Ball Club on Lake Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough stalled over the Southwest States for multiple days while several short waves rotated around the base of the larger trough. This produced periods of severe storms and flooding in Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received of a tree blown onto a carport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall during the month of May resulted in very high river levels on the Red River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall during the month of May in the Red River Basin of Southern Oklahoma, Northern Texas and Southwest Arkansas resulted in very high water on the Red River bordering McCurtain County in Southeast Oklahoma, Little River, Hempstead, Lafayette and Miller Counties in Southwest Arkansas, Bowie and Red River Counties in Northeast Texas as well as Caddo, Bossier, Red River, Natchitoches, Winn and Grant Parishes in Northwest Louisiana. Rainfall amounts during the month of May across the Upper and Middle Red River basins of Southern Oklahoma and Northern Texas totaled over 20 inches of rain.  At Shreveport, Louisiana, the Red River rose above the 30 foot flood stage on May 30th and remained above flood stage through the end of the month. The Red River in Shreveport, Louisiana, which borders Caddo and Bossier Parishes in Northwest Louisiana, did not crest until the month of June, 2015. A more complete write up for the Red River in Northwest Louisiana will be included in the June, 2015 Storm Data publication.  Below Shreveport, Louisiana at Coushatta, Louisiana, the Red River rose above the 31 foot flood stage on May 18th and remained above flood stage through the end of the month.  The Red River at Coushatta, Louisiana did not crest until the month of June, 2015 and a more complete write up for the Red River in Red River Parish will be included in the June, 2015 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low over the southern Rockies aided in the development of surface low pressure over north Texas on the 26th. As this low moved east along a stationary front over Louisiana and Mississippi, a strong line of thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of the 27th. This line of storms moved rapidly across southeast Louisiana during the daylight hours of the 27th. Numerous reports of thunderstorm wind damage were received, primarily downed trees and power lines. Several meso-cyclones embedded in the southern potion of the squall line spawned weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado., along the leading edge of a squall line,  touched down at the levee of Duncan Canal north of West Esplanade Avenue. Two wooden power poles were bent near an apartment complex. The storm tracked to the east-southeast with mainly light tree damage, and one area of light roof damage on Grandlake Boulevard. The tornado then crossed Canal Number 17, and lifted the roof off of the back porch of a house on Tulane Drive. On the opposite side of Tulane Drive, another house had a small portion of its roof ripped off. The tornado continued to the east-southeast and crossed West Louisiana State Drive and bent over one wooden power pole, and snapped a second one. The tornado then uprooted a hardwood tree in front of a house on West Loyola. The most significant damage occurred at the corner of Loyola and West Esplanade, where multiple wooden power poles and a few trees were snapped. The tornado then quickly lifted as it crossed West Esplanade. In addition to the tornado, sporadic light tree damage occurred south of the track. A large portion of the facade of a hotel on the corner of Loyola and the Interstate 10 Service Road was damaged due to straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall during the month of May resulted in very high river levels on the Red River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall during the month of May in the Red River Basin of Southern Oklahoma, Northern Texas and Southwest Arkansas resulted in very high water on the Red River bordering McCurtain County in Southeast Oklahoma, Little River, Hempstead, Lafayette and Miller Counties in Southwest Arkansas, Bowie and Red River Counties in Northeast Texas as well as Caddo, Bossier, Red River, Natchitoches, Winn and Grant Parishes in Northwest Louisiana. Rainfall amounts during the month of May across the Upper and Middle Red River basins of Southern Oklahoma and Northern Texas totaled over 20 inches of rain. At Shreveport, Louisiana, the Red River rose above the 30 foot flood stage on May 30th and remained above flood stage through the end of the month. The Red River in Shreveport, Louisiana, which borders Caddo and Bossier Parishes in Northwest Louisiana, did not crest until the month of June, 2015. A more complete write up for the Red River in Northwest Louisiana will be included in the June, 2015 Storm Data publication. Below Shreveport, Louisiana at Coushatta, Louisiana, the Red River rose above the 31 foot flood stage on May 18th and remained above flood stage through the end of the month. The Red River at Coushatta, Louisiana did not crest until the month of June, 2015 and a more complete write up for the Red River in Red River Parish will be included in the June, 2015 Storm Data publication. Below Coushatta, Louisiana at Grand Ecore, Louisiana, the Red River rose above the 33 foot flood stage on May 18th and remained above flood stage through the end of the month. The Red River at Grand Ecore, Louisiana did not crest until the month of June, 2015. A more complete write up for the Red River in Grant Parish will be included in the June, 2015 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of August 19th over Northeast Texas and Northwest Louisiana, along a weak cold front that had moved into the area. Many of these storms merged with one another over Harrison County in East Texas and across Caddo and Bossier Parishes in Northwest Louisiana, as they moved repeatedly along Interstate 20 between Waskom, Texas and Bossier City, Louisiana. These storms produced hourly rain rates between 2 and 3 inches per hour, with widespread storm totals of 3 to nearly 6 inches, especially between Waskom and Greenwood. This resulted in flash flooding of roads in both of these cities. In addition, some of these storms produced locally gusty winds, with a microburst with winds of around 60 mph downing several trees and snapping several large tree limbs in far South Shreveport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were flooded in and around the Greenwood community.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesocyclone embedded in the squall line produced another tornado initially touching down in an area of softwood pine trees west of Bayouside Street. Trees had large branches snapped, and EF-0 damage was noted. The path width was only 25 yards at this point. The tornado then moved to the southeast and crossed Louisiana Highway 402 causing additional EF-0 damage. Several trees had large branches snapped off, and a few trees were uprooted. No visible damage to any structures was noted.  The path width in this area was approximately 75 yards wide. The tornado crossed Louisiana Highway 1 and Bayou Lafourche just south of Hospital Road, causing additional tree damage. The tornado then crossed Louisiana Highway 308 south of Dodge Road, where additional tree damage in the form of large branches removed from hardwood trees was noted. All of this damage was rated EF-0, and the path width narrowed to 25 yards. No damage was noted beyond this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southeast Louisiana provided the focus for the development of several rounds of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Torrential rain produced several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm spotter observed stalled vehicles on Highway 1 due to water covered roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low over the southern Rockies aided in the development of surface low pressure over north Texas on the 26th. As this low moved east along a stationary front over Louisiana and Mississippi, a strong line of thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of the 27th. This line of storms moved rapidly across southeast Louisiana during the daylight hours of the 27th. Numerous reports of thunderstorm wind damage were received, primarily downed trees and power lines. Several meso-cyclones embedded in the southern potion of the squall line spawned weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another embedded mesocyclone embedded in the squall line produced a tornado. Tree damage was noted across a field west of Louisiana Highway 20, and due to lack of road access, this was determined to be the start point of the tornado. Several large trees were uprooted along the tree line. Damage was noted as EF-1 in this location. The tornado then moved east-southeast across the field, and crossed Louisiana Highway 20 at the intersection with Appletree Drive. EF-1 damage was noted here, and the tornado was approximately 75 yards wide at this point. A small professional building had large portions of siding ripped off and shingles missing from the roof. Trees were uprooted in varying directions in front of the home, and several power poles were snapped at the base in this location. The tornado then moved east-southeast down Appletree Lane and removed a large portion of the shingles from several homes. Several trees were also uprooted and snapped in this area. High end EF-0 and low end EF-1 damage was noted along Appletree Drive. At the end of Appletree Drive, a detached garage collapsed. The tornado then continued into the woods where a path was noted for several more yards with trees uprooted and snapped. The tornado remained 75 yards wide and maintained EF-1 strength as it moved into the woods. It is unknown if the tornado continued further into the woods due to a lack of access.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low over the southern Rockies aided in the development of surface low pressure over north Texas on the 26th. As this low moved east along a stationary front over Louisiana and Mississippi, a strong line of thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of the 27th. This line of storms moved rapidly across southeast Louisiana during the daylight hours of the 27th. Numerous reports of thunderstorm wind damage were received, primarily downed trees and power lines. Several meso-cyclones embedded in the southern potion of the squall line spawned weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesocyclone embedded in the squall line produced a weak tornado. Small trees were uprooted in a field west of Louisiana Highway 306, producing EF-0 damage. Path width was 25 yards wide at this point. The tornado quickly moved to the east-southeast and crossed Highway 306 between Mark and Matthew Streets, where additional large tree branches were snapped from numerous trees. Minor damage to roof coverings was noted along Mark and Matthew Streets. Power poles were also leaning south of Matthew Street. The damage path was 50 yards wide at this point and EF-0 damage was noted. The tornado continued east-southeast, and moved across Shamrock Drive at Edna Street. A home had siding removed and some shingles removed. Trees were also damaged along Edna Street. The tornado was 50 yards wide and produced EF-0 damage along Edna and Shamrock Streets. There was additional tree damage observed beyond the levee behind Shamrock Street, but due to lack of access, this is where the end point of the tornado is noted.

Strong north winds behind a cold front that passed during the 3rd pushed the tide along the coast to less than 1 foot below MLLW during the 4th and 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds along the coast pushed the tide at Calcasieu Pass below 1 foot below MLLW during two consecutive low tides. A cold, arctic airmass entered the region from the northwest during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Wednesday, March 4th. The flow aloft was from the west southwest and disturbances embedded in this flow produced the lift necessary for precipitation to overspread the region from west to east. The precipitation began as a cold rain but quickly transitioned to sleet during the late night hours of March 4th with the precipitation transitioning over to a mixture of sleet and snow during the morning hours of March 5th.  Along and north of the Interstate 20 corridor of Northern Louisiana, freezing rain amounts were mainly less than one tenth of an inch with sleet amounts near one half to one inch with snow amounts ranging from trace amounts to near one inch accumulations.  ||South of the Interstate 20 corridor of Northern Louisiana, freezing rain, sleet and snow accumulations were much less.

This boundary served as the focus for development of severe thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and coastal Mississippi during the afternoon hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The water level at Cameron fell below -1 below lower low water for three and a half hours reaching -1.36 during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts as they moved across Northeast Louisiana. The storms weakened during the evening hours over the eastern portions of Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts as they moved across Northeast Louisiana. The storms weakened during the evening hours over the eastern portions of Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail occurred along with some very gusty winds which brought down some small limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same upper level trough that was responsible for excessive heavy rainfall across Northeast Texas on March 9th remained nearly stationary across West Texas on March 12th.  An area of low pressure at the surface developed off the Southeast Texas and Southwest Louisiana Coasts.  Moisture continued to return rapidly into the region from the south and southwest in response to the upper level trough and the surface low pressure area.  A narrow line of convection developed during the predawn hours of March 12th and moved rapidly northward into Northwest Louisiana.  As a result, excessive heavy rainfall produced flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drainage ditch completely caved in from flood waters prompting voluntary evacuations from the Normandy Village in South Highland neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, arctic airmass entered the region from the northwest during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Wednesday, March 4th. The flow aloft was from the west southwest and disturbances embedded in this flow produced the lift necessary for precipitation to overspread the region from west to east. The precipitation began as a cold rain but quickly transitioned to sleet during the late night hours of March 4th with the precipitation transitioning over to a mixture of sleet and snow during the morning hours of March 5th.  Along and north of the Interstate 20 corridor of Northern Louisiana, freezing rain amounts were mainly less than one tenth of an inch with sleet amounts near one half to one inch with snow amounts ranging from trace amounts to near one inch accumulations.  ||South of the Interstate 20 corridor of Northern Louisiana, freezing rain, sleet and snow accumulations were much less.

A stationary front over southeast Louisiana provided the focus for the development of several rounds of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Torrential rain produced several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported across the northbound lanes of Airport Road in Slidell. One half foot of street flooding was reported in the Ozone Woods neighborhood. Grouse Street near Jay Street was flooded with 1 to 2 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stationary frontal boundary, rich gulf moisture, instability and day time heating triggered some strong to severe storms. There storms caused a significant wind damage across Northeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was recorded at Tallulah-Vicksburg airport automated observing system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of wintry weather had occurred prior to this snowfall event. A cold front had moved through the region four days earlier on February 21st with a cold airmass in its wake. Several waves of upper level disturbances moved through Mid-South, over the course of the next three days. The first, on the night of the 22nd, brought mostly rain to the region but some light icing occurred in the far northern Delta early on the 23rd. A second, more potent disturbance, moved through Central Mississippi during the afternoon and evening hours on the 23rd. This brought a more significant icing event to locations generally along and north of I-20, causing some power outages and accidents. ||With the cold remaining in place, the final round of wintry weather moved in on the morning of the 25th. A strong upper level disturbance moved across the region, which induced a low pressure system to move east across the northern Gulf of Mexico. With the cold air already entrenched over the region, this brought the needed moisture and atmospheric lift needed to generate precipitation. At first, the precipitation started as rain and freezing rain, with some light icing reported across the ArkLaMiss. As the atmosphere cooled through the late morning, the rain began to change to snow in the early afternoon across southeast Arkansas, northeast Louisiana and the Mississippi Delta. The changeover line from rain to snow slowly progressed from northwest to southeast across northern portions of the ArkLaMiss region. By the time the changeover occurred near the I-20 corridor, the precipitation was moving off to the east into Alabama. ||Those who got snow north of I-20 saw several heavier bursts, which led to some high snowfall totals. The highest totals were generally along and north of the Highway 82 corridor. Locations from Grenada to northern Lowndes County saw the highest amounts in our county warning area, with totals ranging from six to eight inches. Those who saw the higher totals also dealt with trees being weighed down by the heavy snow. This led to snapping trees and numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three and a half inches of snow fell in the parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over South Lake Charles and slowly drifted east. While the storm developed a funnel cloud developed near the Lake Charles Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over South Lake Charles during the early afternoon. As the storm grew a funnel cloud developed just north of the Lake Charles Regional Airport. Numerous National Weather Service employees watched the funnel as it drifted east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into far Southeast Texas and Southwest Louisiana and stalled. As the boundary stalled heavy rain fell across sections of Jefferson and Orange Counties and Calcasieu Parish.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorm trained across Jefferson and Orange Counties and western Calcasieu Parish for multiple hours. This caused flooding around the town of Vinton and closed some streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass moved into Louisiana during the first week of March behind an arctic cold front. During the morning of the 5th an upper level disturbance moved across the region producing a light dusting of snow and sleet across sections of Central Louisiana. Sleet fell all the way south to the Interstate 10 corridor, however no accumulation was noted across South Louisiana. Along the coast, strong north winds drove out the tides for around a full day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures and reports from Facebook indicated around half an inch of snow and sleet fell across Leesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleet and snow fell across Rapides Parish during the morning of the 5th. Alexandria International Airport and surrounding areas received one quarter inch of accumulation. During and after the event the Highway 8 Bridge in Boyce was closed for several hours from icing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around half an inch of snow and sleet fell across portions of Avoyelles Parish including Marksville and Bordelonville. Accumulation information was received through Facebook.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tide stations and river gauges from Cameron and the Calcasieu River show tide levels fell below 1 foot below MLLW during the morning of the 5th and did not rise above -1  until the morning of the 6th in Calcasieu Lake. The tide at the coast did rise above -1 during high tide. The water level fell to -2.16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over southeast Louisiana provided the focus for the development of several rounds of thunderstorms over southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi. Torrential rain produced several reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3 year old fell into a drainage canal and was swept away by a strong current due to flooding rains. The child's body was recovered about 75 yards downstream about 15 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft across the southwest United States resulted in broad southwest flow aloft across the Southern Plains into the Lower Mississippi Valley. Disturbances in this flow rounded the base of the trough and provided the lift necessary for widespread showers and thunderstorms across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana. A cold front was located across Northern Texas into Northern Louisiana. Moderate surface instability coupled with strong deep layer shear allowed the storms that developed to produce strong and damaging wind gusts in a few locations of Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the Pollock community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stationary frontal boundary, rich gulf moisture, instability and day time heating triggered some strong to severe storms. There storms caused a significant wind damage across Northeast Louisiana.

With only a little over a week since the previous heavy snowfall event on February 25th, the region again saw another winter weather maker. Luckily for most of the area, the region had begun a warmup that helped alleviate a lot of road issues. After cold high pressure had moved just to the north of the region around March 2nd, a warm front surged north, bringing moisture and much warmer temperatures back into the region. Temperatures warmed quickly on March 4th into the mid 70s to low 80s but a change was in store. A cold front moved quickly to the southeast due to a very cold and strong high pressure building through southern Canada and northern Plains towards our region. Temperatures quickly plummeted around 40 to 50 degrees in the wake of the cold front.||As the cold front quickly moved southeast, colder air continued to filter in. However, moisture lingered behind the front. Rain began to change over to sleet late the night of March 4th into early morning hours of March 5th. As colder air in the lower parts of the atmosphere continued to filter in, moisture began to transition over to a wintry mix, mainly in the form of sleet, in the northwest Delta. This setup was very favorable for a transition from rain to freezing rain to sleet. Moisture further southeast near the Natchez Trace corridor and Jackson metro began to transition over in the early morning hours of March 5th, with some moderate sleet falling in central Mississippi. Sleet and some snow began to mix into the Delta while the majority of the area saw a significant accumulation of sleet. Some areas in south and east Mississippi also saw some sleet but a little to no accumulation. Precipitation moved out late morning of March 5th.||Those who received a good amount of sleet and some snow accumulation (mainly along and northwest of the Natchez Trace corridor) saw anywhere between 1/2 inch to 1-2 inches in the far northern parts of the Delta and northern Mississippi. Due to being warm the two previous days, road issues were mainly limited to areas that received significant heavy sleet accumulation, mainly in the Delta and northern Mississippi. These areas saw numerous roads that were ice covered and very slick, making travel very hazardous. Areas in the Jackson Metro saw some slick roads but warm ground and road temperature from the previous few days alleviated some of the issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one half inch of heavy sleet accumulated at Newlight.

Areas in the Jackson Metro saw some slick roads but warm ground and road temperature from the previous few days alleviated some of the issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one inch of heavy sleet accumulated in Delhi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With only a little over a week since the previous heavy snowfall event on February 25th, the region again saw another winter weather maker. Luckily for most of the area, the region had begun a warmup that helped alleviate a lot of road issues. After cold high pressure had moved just to the north of the region around March 2nd, a warm front surged north, bringing moisture and much warmer temperatures back into the region. Temperatures warmed quickly on March 4th into the mid 70s to low 80s but a change was in store. A cold front moved quickly to the southeast due to a very cold and strong high pressure building through southern Canada and northern Plains towards our region. Temperatures quickly plummeted around 40 to 50 degrees in the wake of the cold front.||As the cold front quickly moved southeast, colder air continued to filter in. However, moisture lingered behind the front. Rain began to change over to sleet late the night of March 4th into early morning hours of March 5th. As colder air in the lower parts of the atmosphere continued to filter in, moisture began to transition over to a wintry mix, mainly in the form of sleet, in the northwest Delta. This setup was very favorable for a transition from rain to freezing rain to sleet. Moisture further southeast near the Natchez Trace corridor and Jackson metro began to transition over in the early morning hours of March 5th, with some moderate sleet falling in central Mississippi. Sleet and some snow began to mix into the Delta while the majority of the area saw a significant accumulation of sleet. Some areas in south and east Mississippi also saw some sleet but a little to no accumulation. Precipitation moved out late morning of March 5th.||Those who received a good amount of sleet and some snow accumulation (mainly along and northwest of the Natchez Trace corridor) saw anywhere between 1/2 inch to 1-2 inches in the far northern parts of the Delta and northern Mississippi. Due to being warm the two previous days, road issues were mainly limited to areas that received significant heavy sleet accumulation, mainly in the Delta and northern Mississippi. These areas saw numerous roads that were ice covered and very slick, making travel very hazardous. Areas in the Jackson Metro saw some slick roads but warm ground and road temperature from the previous few days alleviated some of the issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one half inch of sleet accumulated in Tallulah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft across the southwest United States resulted in broad southwest flow aloft across the Southern Plains into the Lower Mississippi Valley. Disturbances in this flow rounded the base of the trough and provided the lift necessary for widespread showers and thunderstorms across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana. A cold front was located across Northern Texas into Northern Louisiana. Moderate surface instability coupled with strong deep layer shear allowed the storms that developed to produce strong and damaging wind gusts in a few locations of Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the Williana community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall was widespread across Northern Louisiana during the morning hours of May 18th.  This convection developed from an upper level disturbance that moved into the region from Central Texas.  Moisture was plentiful across the region as the upper level flow was from the southwest.  This allowed the disturbance to tap moisture from not only the Eastern Pacific but also the Gulf of Mexico.  These storms trained across the same general area resulting in some impressive rainfall totals.  The end result was widespread flooding across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Cory Road near Hwy. 165 north of Columbia, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough stalled over the southwest states while multiple short waves rotated around the larger trough over a few days. This produced multiple rounds of severe weather with large hail and high winds reported. The first round entered Louisiana by early afternoon on the 24th as a long lived super cell tracked from Beauregard Parish and across Acadiana then into the gulf. The second round of severe weather produced a couple reports of wind damage while the 3rd and 4th rounds produced widespread wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager of Beauregard Parish reported trees down between Fields and Ragley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist air mass combined with afternoon heating and good deep shear to develop strong to severe storms during the night of the 18th. During the overnight hours from the 19th to 20th, a strong upper disturbance combined with afternoon heating, moist airmass, cold front and deep shear to cause more strong to severe storms. These storms produced hail up to golf ball size in portions of Northeast Louisiana. There was also thunderstorm wind damage in the form of blown down trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a stationary frontal boundary, rich gulf moisture, instability and day time heating triggered some strong to severe storms. There storms caused a significant wind damage across Northeast Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a swath of damaging winds in this area. There was substantial tree damage in the area from Highway 165 and along and north of Highway 554. Three businesses were damaged with a roof off one and a portable office building blown across the road and destroyed. A vehicle was blown off the road and trees fell on the car. A lady sustain minor injuries. Multiple trees were blown down with some power poles snapped across the south part of the parish. Thirty to thirty-five homes sustained some sort of damage to shingles off the roof to the homes damaged by trees. A pontoon boat and a trailer was also flipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning line of thunderstorms produced a weak tornado in Southwest Louisiana as a short wave passed through the region. A drier air mass moved into the region by the afternoon, however another short wave affected the region during the afternoon with more thunderstorms. Storms in Central Louisiana produced hail while one decaying storm over the coastal gulf waters lead to a heat burst over Southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A decaying thunderstorm passed southeast across the Jefferson County coast in Southeast Texas and into the near shore gulf waters of Cameron Parish. A heat burst occurred to the north of this storm across southern and central sections of Calcasieu Parish. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 MPH were common with a one second gust of 74 MPH recorded on top of the Magnolia Building in Downtown Lake Charles by KPLC 7. The ASOS at the Lake Charles Regional Airport recorded 51 MPH and Chennault Field recorded 54 MPH. Many trees were downed in the city as were power lines. Mostly light damage was noted to some structures with one dock at a city park damaged from wave action on Prien Lake. After the event some sections of the city were with out power for 3 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late on the evening of Friday, April 24th into the early morning hours of Saturday, April 25th, a complex of severe thunderstorms pushed eastward across northeastern Louisiana and central Mississippi. These storms were produced by a shortwave trough pushing across the Mid South region and were sustained by strong deep layer wind shear. Damaging straight-line wind gusts and a few weak tornadoes occurred along the path of the storms. Several trees and power poles/lines were downed, some onto structures. A few areas in the ArkLaMiss Delta also received very heavy rain producing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Bastrop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall was widespread across Northern Louisiana during the morning hours of May 18th.  This convection developed from an upper level disturbance that moved into the region from Central Texas.  Moisture was plentiful across the region as the upper level flow was from the southwest.  This allowed the disturbance to tap moisture from not only the Eastern Pacific but also the Gulf of Mexico.  These storms trained across the same general area resulting in some impressive rainfall totals.  The end result was widespread flooding across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over McCann Road in the Trout community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough stalled over the southwest states while multiple short waves rotated around the larger trough over a few days. This produced multiple rounds of severe weather with large hail and high winds reported. The first round entered Louisiana by early afternoon on the 24th as a long lived super cell tracked from Beauregard Parish and across Acadiana then into the gulf. The second round of severe weather produced a couple reports of wind damage while the 3rd and 4th rounds produced widespread wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado formed in Newton County and moved east to southeast across the Sabine River. The tornado passed near Bancroft before dissipating near Fields. Mostly tree damage occurred along the path, however one home was damaged when a tree fell onto the roof and a couple of outbuildings were also damaged near Bancroft. The path in Beauregard Parish was 8.36 miles long with a total path distance of  12.3 miles. The average width was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning line of thunderstorms produced a weak tornado in Southwest Louisiana as a short wave passed through the region. A drier air mass moved into the region by the afternoon, however another short wave affected the region during the afternoon with more thunderstorms. Storms in Central Louisiana produced hail while one decaying storm over the coastal gulf waters lead to a heat burst over Southwest Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A decaying thunderstorm passed southeast across the Jefferson County coast in Southeast Texas and into the near shore gulf waters of Cameron Parish. A heat burst occurred to the north of this storm from Calcasieu and Cameron Parishes into central sections of Jefferson Davis County. Wind gusts of 45 to 55 MPH were common with the highest wind gust of 63 MPH recorded at the Jennings Airport. Trees and power lines were reported down in the parish.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with a moist airmass to bring storms during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 17th. Storms continued in association to a mesoscale convective vortex late on the 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th, bringing wind damage to portions of southwest Mississippi. Additional storms developed across Louisiana and west Mississippi through the night. These storms were extremely slow moving and continued to develop and move over the same areas, which resulted in significant flash flooding across portions of northeast Louisiana and west Mississippi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall of 6.24 inches fell at the Tallulah-Vicksburg Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with ample moisture to bring scattered storms to the region. Isolated severe storms brought some small hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor roof damage occurred. Several trees about 18 inches in diameter were uprooted and took down power lines. Some of these power lines were partially laying on Highway 124. Penny size hail also fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of low pressure moved along a stationary front triggering two rounds of thunderstorms that produced severe weather. The first moved across southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of the 24th. The second moved across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi during the late morning and afternoon hours of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weatherflow observation at the foot of Sunset Point Pier measured 50.3 knot wind gust or 58 mph. Numerous large limbs were reported blown down along with some fences in the old Mandeville area. Electric utility customers reported over 800 customer without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete supercell thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours across North Central Texas. These storms moved eastward into Northeast Texas during the late evening and overnight hours of the 18th. By the time the storms moved into Northeast Texas, the storms had evolved into a broken line of storms...producing damaging wind gusts and large hail.  This line of storms continued to propagate eastward into Northwest Louisiana and produced sporadic wind damage in the form of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Hwy. 9 in Arcadia, Louisiana.

Widespread wind damage was reported across the region during the afternoon and evening hours with flash flooding reported across portions of the region as well with a few tornadoes embedded in the line of thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances in this flow allowed for vigorous thunderstorm growth across Central and North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 24th. A warm front that was nearly stationary across North Texas into West Central Louisiana began moving north during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for a warm sector airmass to move into the entire region. Moderate instability, strong deep layer shear and high lapse rates resulted in multiple modes of strong to severe thunderstorms across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the Mooringsport community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of low pressure moved along a stationary front triggering two rounds of thunderstorms that produced severe weather. The first moved across southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of the 24th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on East Judge Perez between Center and Alexander Streets in Arabi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same weather system that produced flash flooding along the Mississippi Gulf Coast on the evening of the 16th aided the development of isolated thunderstorms north of Lake Pontchartrain early in the morning of the 17th. At least one tornado was reported just west of the town of Tickfaw.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Sibley Road about 1/4 mile east of Woodhaven Road, uprooting two hardwood trees. The track continued north-northwest with damage increasing significantly once the tornado reached Joel McDonald Road. On the far east and south side of the road, two houses had damage to their metal roofs with several sections missing. Barns and sheds in the yards of those homes were no longer intact. A metal roof from one of the sheds was found in a tree just above power line height. Across the street from these two homes were two mobile homes, both parallel to Joel McDonald Road. One mobile home was rolled onto its roof and the other was pushed off its blocks. Both were strapped down, with some straps snapping while others remained attached to the anchors which were uprooted. The tornado continued north-northwest toward Gene McDonald Road. Several trees were uprooted near homes on that road. Front and back porches on two mobile homes were lifted and thrown approximately 30 yards. One mobile home had a 3 foot by 6 foot hole in an exterior wall blown from the inside out. Two sheds were destroyed. The tornado lifted after uprooting two trees near Miller Lane Road. One was on the west side, the other on the east side of the road. One of those trees hit a pickup truck. Maximum wind speed was estimated at 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating and instability aided the development of several thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 19th. One report of severe weather was received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed a report from the public of dime to quarter size hail at Interstate 110 and Chippewa Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm, moist, and unstable air was in place during the afternoon of Wednesday, April 1st, as an upper level trough of low pressure traversed east across Southeast Oklahoma, Western Arkansas, and East Texas.  Increased large scale forcing within this unstable environment ahead of the trough resulted in the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms over portions of Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and North Central Louisiana during the mid-afternoon and evening hours, with several reports of trees being blown down across these areas.  These storms weakened during the late evening with the loss of instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Gladeway Road east of Jonesboro, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved eastward across the Southern Plains during the late afternoon hours of April 19th. Accompanying the upper level disturbance was a dry line that encountered strong instability across Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana. Once the cap was broken in the afternoon hours, deep layer shear values near 40kts were supportive of discrete supercell thunderstorms. Lapse rates were dry adiabatic and given the low wet bulb zero heights across the region, large hail was the main threat with these storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with a moist airmass to bring storms during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 17th. Storms continued in association to a mesoscale convective vortex late on the 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th, bringing wind damage to portions of southwest Mississippi. Additional storms developed across Louisiana and west Mississippi through the night. These storms were extremely slow moving and continued to develop and move over the same areas, which resulted in significant flash flooding across portions of northeast Louisiana and west Mississippi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances in this flow allowed for vigorous thunderstorm growth across Central and North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 24th. A warm front that was nearly stationary across North Texas into West Central Louisiana began moving north during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for a warm sector airmass to move into the entire region. Moderate instability, strong deep layer shear and high lapse rates resulted in multiple modes of strong to severe thunderstorms across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped and a small tree was uprooted at the corner of Duckwater Landing and Swan Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold dome of arctic air spilled southward out of the Central and Southern Plains, into the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of the Winter Storm event. The flow aloft was from the west southwest with embedded disturbances moving towards the region from West Texas. These disturbances provided the necessary lift to generate widespread winter precipitation across the region in the form of freezing rain and sleet. Temperatures during the predawn hours of February 23rd were mostly just above freezing but once the precipitation moved in from the west, the precipitation quickly changed over to freezing rain mixed with sleet as the temperatures fell during the day. Freezing rain accumulations were mainly less than one quarter of an inch while sleet accumulations ranged from near one half inch to just under one and one half inch across Caddo, Bossier and Webster Parishes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of April 22nd across portions of the region. The atmosphere was characterized by west northwest flow aloft with disturbances in this flow providing the upper level support necessary. A warm front was moving northward across Northeast Texas into Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana at the time as well. Moderate surface instability was present in the warm sector along and south of the northward moving warm front with steep lapse rates. The result was some wind damage and hail with the thunderstorms that developed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic air remained in place across the region after an intrusion of arctic air from earlier in the week. An upper level trough exited the Four Corners region of the country and moved into the Texas Hill Country during the predawn hours of Wednesday, February 25th. Widespread precipitation developed ahead of the trough across Texas and moved into the region shortly after midnight on the 25th. The precipitation began as a mixture of light rain or freezing rain after midnight towards the predawn hours on Wednesday. As the trough moved closer into the region from the west, the precipitation quickly transitioned over to sleet and some light snow after sunrise on the 25th. The mixed winter precipitation moved out of the region during the late afternoon or early evening hours of the 25th. Light Freezing rain, sleet and snow amounts were minimal across the region during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across far west Texas during the early morning hours of May 25th and this cluster of severe thunderstorms evolved into a derecho, moving eastward across the entire state of Texas and eventually into Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana. Widespread wind damage was reported across the region during the afternoon and evening hours with flash flooding reported across portions of the region as well with a few tornadoes embedded in the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs over 20 foot long were downed on Pine Grove Street in southwest Shreveport.

Some flash flooding was also produced with these storms as rainfall rates were excessive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very large limbs fell on a garage roof causing damage to the roof of a Shreveport, Louisiana resident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of April 22nd across portions of the region. The atmosphere was characterized by west northwest flow aloft with disturbances in this flow providing the upper level support necessary. A warm front was moving northward across Northeast Texas into Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana at the time as well. Moderate surface instability was present in the warm sector along and south of the northward moving warm front with steep lapse rates. The result was some wind damage and hail with the thunderstorms that developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team concluded that the damage north of Farmerville, Louisiana was consistent with that of straight line wind gusts to near 80 mph.  Damage was very sporadic and consisted of uprooted and/or snapped trees near the community of Conway, Louisiana.  Several chicken houses suffered moderate to severe damage when the roof was lifted or peeled back, causing the exterior walls to collapse.  All other structural damage consisted of trees falling on homes.  The storm moved southeast across the eastern half of the parish with scattered trees downed near Marion, Haile and Spencer communities.  Approximately 5 to 10 homes across the northern and eastern part of the parish suffered minor to moderate damage from fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold dome of arctic air spilled southward out of the Central and Southern Plains, into the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of the Winter Storm event. The flow aloft was from the west southwest with embedded disturbances moving towards the region from West Texas. These disturbances provided the necessary lift to generate widespread winter precipitation across the region in the form of freezing rain and sleet. Temperatures during the predawn hours of February 23rd were mostly just above freezing but once the precipitation moved in from the west, the precipitation quickly changed over to freezing rain mixed with sleet as the temperatures fell during the day. Freezing rain and sleet accumulations were mainly less than one tenth of an inch across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of April 22nd across portions of the region. The atmosphere was characterized by west northwest flow aloft with disturbances in this flow providing the upper level support necessary. A warm front was moving northward across Northeast Texas into Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana at the time as well. Moderate surface instability was present in the warm sector along and south of the northward moving warm front with steep lapse rates. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team determined that the damage across extreme northern part of Ouachita Parish was consistent with that of straight line wind damage with winds speeds near 80 mph.  The worst damage was found in and around the community of Sterlington, Louisiana.  Damage consisted on many uprooted and/or snapped trees.  5 homes were destroyed with some 20 to 30 additional homes suffering minor to moderate damage from fallen trees.  The damage was concentrated around the Lake Bartholomew area or between Sterlington and the Perryville community.  There was one injury reported when an elderly woman suffered severe cuts and bruises when a tree fell on her home and she became trapped under the front porch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same upper level trough that was responsible for excessive heavy rainfall across Northeast Texas on March 9th remained nearly stationary across West Texas on March 12th.  An area of low pressure at the surface developed off the Southeast Texas and Southwest Louisiana Coasts.  Moisture continued to return rapidly into the region from the south and southwest in response to the upper level trough and the surface low pressure area.  A narrow line of convection developed during the predawn hours of March 12th and moved rapidly northward into Northwest Louisiana.  As a result, excessive heavy rainfall produced flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall resulted in the closure of Red Bluff Road just east of Jessie Latin Road southeast of Stonewall, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold dome of arctic air spilled southward out of the Central and Southern Plains, into the Lower Mississippi Valley ahead of the Winter Storm event. The flow aloft was from the west southwest with embedded disturbances moving towards the region from West Texas. These disturbances provided the necessary lift to generate widespread winter precipitation across the region in the form of freezing rain and sleet. Temperatures during the predawn hours of February 23rd were mostly just above freezing but once the precipitation moved in from the west, the precipitation quickly changed over to freezing rain mixed with sleet as the temperatures fell during the day. Freezing rain accumulations were mainly less than one quarter of an inch while sleet accumulations ranged from near one half inch to just under one and one half inch across Caddo, Bossier and Webster Parishes.

A high moisture content air mass moved back into the region while an upper level short wave moved across Texas and Louisiana. The resulting slow moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding over sections of Central Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused many roads to flood with up to one foot of water across portions of the Alexandria area. Media passed along a report of two cars under water at an underpass at Jackson Street. Flood waters also entered a furniture store and a few homes in Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A teenage boy was playing in flood waters in South Central Louisiana when he was sucked into a drain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Loreauville teen was playing in a flooded yard when he fell and was sucked into drain pipe. The teen was recovered 90 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air combined with an upper level disturbance to bring scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to the area. Some of these storms were severe and brought gusty winds to the region, resulting in some downed trees and other types of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Highway 603.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent stationary front provided the focus for numerous thunderstorms across southern Mississippi and southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of the 15th and early morning of the 16th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of baseball size hail was sent to TV station through social media from Kenner. Event time was based on radar.

A large upper level trough stalled over the southwest states while multiple short waves rotated around the larger trough over a few days. This produced multiple rounds of severe weather with large hail and high winds reported. The first round entered Louisiana by early afternoon on the 24th as a long lived super cell tracked from Beauregard Parish and across Acadiana then into the gulf. The second round of severe weather produced a couple reports of wind damage while the 3rd and 4th rounds produced widespread wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media relayed a report of quarter size hail in Singer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances in this flow allowed for vigorous thunderstorm growth across Central and North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 24th. A warm front that was nearly stationary across North Texas into West Central Louisiana began moving north during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for a warm sector airmass to move into the entire region. Moderate instability, strong deep layer shear and high lapse rates resulted in multiple modes of strong to severe thunderstorms across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golfball sized hail was reported in Vivian, Louisiana.

Cold arctic air remained in place across the region after an intrusion of arctic air from earlier in the week. An upper level trough exited the Four Corners region of the country and moved into the Texas Hill Country during the predawn hours of Wednesday, February 25th. Widespread precipitation developed ahead of the trough across Texas and moved into the region shortly after midnight on the 25th. The precipitation began as a mixture of light rain or freezing rain after midnight towards the predawn hours on Wednesday. As the trough moved closer into the region from the west, the precipitation quickly transitioned over to sleet and some light snow after sunrise on the 25th. The mixed winter precipitation moved out of the region during the late afternoon or early evening hours of the 25th. Light Freezing rain, sleet and snow amounts were minimal across the region during the event.

The first moved across southeast Louisiana during the afternoon of the 24th. The second moved across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi during the late morning and afternoon hours of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail reported by the COOP observer at Bayou Sorrel Lock. Event time was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalling cold front provided a focus for thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi during the afternoon hours on the 11th and 12th. Several reports of severe weather were received both afternoons.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pointe Coupee Parish Emergency Manager reported that several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds on Jack Torres Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalling cold front provided a focus for thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana and southern Mississippi during the afternoon hours on the 11th and 12th. Several reports of severe weather were received both afternoons.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pointe Coupee Parish Emergency Manager reported that several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds on Louisiana Highway 976 near Livonia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved through the area during the afternoon of the 15th, developing numerous thunderstorms across southeast Louisiana. One report of severe weather was received from the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was snapped off by thunderstorm winds and damaged a garage in Hammond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across far west Texas during the early morning hours of May 25th and this cluster of severe thunderstorms evolved into a derecho, moving eastward across the entire state of Texas and eventually into Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana. Widespread wind damage was reported across the region during the afternoon and evening hours with flash flooding reported across portions of the region as well with a few tornadoes embedded in the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in West Monroe, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic air remained in place across the region after an intrusion of arctic air from earlier in the week. An upper level trough exited the Four Corners region of the country and moved into the Texas Hill Country during the predawn hours of Wednesday, February 25th. Widespread precipitation developed ahead of the trough across Texas and moved into the region shortly after midnight on the 25th. The precipitation began as a mixture of light rain or freezing rain after midnight towards the predawn hours on Wednesday. As the trough moved closer into the region from the west, the precipitation quickly transitioned over to sleet and some light snow after sunrise on the 25th. The mixed winter precipitation moved out of the region during the late afternoon or early evening hours of the 25th. Light Freezing rain, sleet and snow amounts were minimal across the region during the event.

Very warm, moist, and unstable air was in place during the afternoon of Wednesday, April 1st, as an upper level trough of low pressure traversed east across Southeast Oklahoma, Western Arkansas, and East Texas.  Increased large scale forcing within this unstable environment ahead of the trough resulted in the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms over portions of Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and North Central Louisiana during the mid-afternoon and evening hours, with several reports of trees being blown down across these areas.  These storms weakened during the late evening with the loss of instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Tom Carson Road just west southwest of Calvin, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very warm, moist, and unstable air was in place during the afternoon of Wednesday, April 1st, as an upper level trough of low pressure traversed east across Southeast Oklahoma, Western Arkansas, and East Texas.  Increased large scale forcing within this unstable environment ahead of the trough resulted in the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms over portions of Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and North Central Louisiana during the mid-afternoon and evening hours, with several reports of trees being blown down across these areas.  These storms weakened during the late evening with the loss of instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Hwy. 501 in Calvin, Louisiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough stalled over the southwest states while multiple short waves rotated around the larger trough over a few days. This produced multiple rounds of severe weather with large hail and high winds reported. The first round entered Louisiana by early afternoon on the 24th as a long lived super cell tracked from Beauregard Parish and across Acadiana then into the gulf. The second round of severe weather produced a couple reports of wind damage while the 3rd and 4th rounds produced widespread wind damage and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm over Hayes produce quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West southwest flow aloft with embedded disturbances in this flow allowed for vigorous thunderstorm growth across Central and North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 24th. A warm front that was nearly stationary across North Texas into West Central Louisiana began moving north during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for a warm sector airmass to move into the entire region. Moderate instability, strong deep layer shear and high lapse rates resulted in multiple modes of strong to severe thunderstorms across Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northern Louisiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines were downed in the community of Shongaloo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey team determined that damage in Claiborne Parish was consistent with that of an EF1 tornado.  The tornado first touched down 7 miles east southeast of Haynesville, Louisiana.  Damage consisted of numerous trees uprooted and/or snapped.  Several trees took out powerlines in the area.  One house received major damage as a tree fell on the house.  Winds were estimated to be between 95 and 105 mph.  The path width was 820 yards wide while the path length was 9.29 miles long.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions occurred over Northeast Louisiana during the summer months with drought conditions developing over the area by late summer and continuing into October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very dry conditions continued across Northeast Louisiana in October. The extended dry stretch resulted in an area of extreme (D3) drought developing across the region by October 13th. Approximately 25 to 50 percent of normal rainfall occurred across this area from August into mid October. Crops were put under more stress from the dry and hot conditions. Heavy rainfall fell during the period from October 20th to October 27th, which resulted in the intensity of the drought falling back to Moderate (D1) Drought conditions by October 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from south Texas to southwest Louisiana during the day and night of the 25th. A warm front associated with the low pressure provided the focus for isolated thunderstorms that produced two tornadoes over southeast Louisiana. In addition, persistent strong southeast and winds generated by a strong pressure gradient produced several reports of minor wind damage primarily to trees. Strong onshore wind caused tides to be 1 to 3 feet above normal, but locally higher.  Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred in low lying terrain in coastal areas and near tidal lakes. Very heavy rain also occurred with this system with several locations receiving as much as ten inches of rain over a 48 hour period. However, due to drought conditions prior to this storm, flooding was much less than usual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tides of 2 to 4 feet above normal tide levels resulted in moderate flooding of roadways across southern Terrebonne Parish and many roads were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With D4 Exceptional Drought conditions ongoing across Bienville Parish during the middle of October, the wildfire danger was very high.  An isolated thunderstorm moved across the area on the evening of Monday, October 12th between 9 and 10 pm.  Residents called 911 reporting a lightning strike and fire approximately 4 miles east southeast of Heflin, Louisiana.  A wildfire began from the lightning strike and through Thursday, October 15th, burned between 1000 and 1400 acres of timberland and grassland.  Although two dozen homes were threatened, only one home was lost.  There were no injuries reported.  Another fire began on Tuesday, October 13th approximately 5 miles east southeast of Bienville in Bienville Parish.  This fire, known as the Liberty Hill Fire, burned mostly timerland and grasslands with total acres lost between 700 and 1000 acres.  Although 5 homes and one church was threatened, only one home was lost.  There were no injuries reported.

With D4 Exceptional Drought conditions ongoing across Red River Parish during the middle of October, the wildfire danger was very high.  A wildfire began in the Martin Community during the afternoon of October 15th and quickly spread to near 500 acres.  The fire consumed grassland and timberland.  The location was north of Hwy. 155 between St. Mary Church Road and LA. Hwy. 507.  Five structures were lost including one inhabited dwelling but there were no injuries.  One tractor was also lost.  The fire was 100% contained as of 7 pm on October 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced a squall line that moved through southeast Louisiana during the afternoon hours of the 31st. Two tornadoes occurred and several reports of wind damage were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down just west of Hoover Road, snapping a tree and moved to the northeast. Before reaching Hoover Road, numerous large tree limbs were broken, a barn lost numerous roof panels, and several undercarriage panels of a modular home were ripped off. Sheet metal panels were strewn across the area for about 200 to 300 yards. After crossing Hoover Road, another large tree was snapped and another uprooted. The tornado then crossed US Highway 190 and crossed over Edna Kinchen Road. This location had the most significant tree damage with multiple softwoods and hardwoods snapped and uprooted. The tornado then moved into a field along Crain Road before causing some very light tree limb damage and minor roof damage to a mobile home. The tornado then continued to the northeast, crossing over Hutchinson Cemetary Road causing minor roof damage to another modular house, scattered limb damage, and knocked down a large section of a wood fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front produced a squall line that moved through southeast Louisiana during the afternoon hours of the 31st. Two tornadoes occurred and several reports of wind damage were reported with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Mayers Road where a large tree was uprooted with minor damage to the undercarriage of a modular house, as well as sporadic large tree limb damage. The tornado continued into a wooded area crossing over the Tangipahoa River and Louisiana Highway 443. The tornado continued to the east leading to the most significant damage along Averett Road. Multiple metal sheds were destroyed. A large metal carport was ripped off, peeling some of the roof of a modular home off with it. Portions of this carport were thrown over 250 yards. Power lines were knocked down, along with one snapped tree, and multiple tree limbs down. A trampoline was also thrown 200-300 yards. The tornado continued to the east, crossing over Bailey Loop Road, leading to additional tree damage and one uprooted softwood tree. In addition, multiple smaller metal sheds were destroyed with the metal being carried 100-150 yards. The tornado lifted at Louisiana Highway 445.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado formed in the swampland between Highway 112 and the Calcasieu River. The tornado crossed LA 461 causing damage to trees. Along Elmer Road a house had roof damage. The wide path continued northeast damaging many trees. One house was crushed by a falling tree. The tornado dissipated near the Claiborne Range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with a cold front. Some of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was damage to two homes, with shingle and awning damage on 270 Busby Road. A portion of the roof was blown open, and a camper was rolled over and damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms occurred as a strong low pressure system moved through the region. Some of these storms produced flash flooding and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued to deteriorate across Northwest Louisiana during the first half of October with much of Northern Louisiana being classified as either D2 Severe Drought status or D3 Extreme Drought status.  The month began with Claiborne, Lincoln and Union Parishes classified as D4 Exceptional Drought status. As the month continued, the D4 Exceptional Drought status expanded westward, encompassing Caddo, Bossier, De Soto, Red River, Webster, Bienville, Jackson and Ouachita Parishes.  Towards the last week in October, an upper level storm system moved into the Southern Plains and Lower Mississippi Valley and produced some much needed rainfall to the region. The rainfall was significant enough that by the end of October, drought conditions were downgraded below D2 Severe Drought status across all of Northern Louisiana.

The time of the tornado was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved along the coast of Louisiana water levels rose above 3 feet above MLLW. Minor flooding was reported from Sabine Pass to Cypremort and Burns Points. As the low pressure departed the area north winds pushed water levels below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent onshore flow drove water levels to a foot or two above normal levels. At high tide and while a line of thunderstorms passed through the area water covered Highway 82 near Holly Beach in a few sections.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved along the coast of Louisiana water levels rose above 3 feet above MLLW. Minor flooding was reported from Sabine Pass to Cypremort and Burns Points. As the low pressure departed the area north winds pushed water levels below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent onshore wind drove waters levels along the coast to a foot or two above normal. As a line of thunderstorms moved through the area at high tide water covered portions of Marshal Street in downtown Cameron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved along the coast of Louisiana water levels rose above 3 feet above MLLW. Minor flooding was reported from Sabine Pass to Cypremort and Burns Points. As the low pressure departed the area north winds pushed water levels below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent onshore flow drove water levels a foot or two above normal levels,  however a line of thunderstorms pushed through during the afternoon of the 25th lifting them higher. The tide gauge at Freshwater Lock hit 6 feet with the passing storms. The operator of the lock said the tide actually hit 6.8 feet on the gulf side of the lock and quickly receded. The high water flooded the  marsh, the yard around the lock, and nearby docks.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures posted to social media showed streets around Cypremort and Burns Points flooded around the time of high tide. Tides were driven up even more when a line of thunderstorms pass through the area.

As the month continued, the D4 Exceptional Drought status expanded westward, encompassing Caddo, Bossier, De Soto, Red River, Webster, Bienville, Jackson and Ouachita Parishes.  Towards the last week in October, an upper level storm system moved into the Southern Plains and Lower Mississippi Valley and produced some much needed rainfall to the region. The rainfall was significant enough that by the end of October, drought conditions were downgraded below D2 Severe Drought status across all of Northern Louisiana.

With D4 Exceptional Drought conditions ongoing across Natchitoches Parish during the middle of October, the wildfire danger was very high.  A downed powerline sparked a wildfire near the Red River/Natchitoches Parish line in the vicinity of Coffee Crossing Road.  The fire burned nearly 1500 acres of timberland.  No structures were damaged and the fire was 100% contained as of 945 pm on October 17th.

Low pressure moved from south Texas to southwest Louisiana during the day and night of the 25th. A warm front associated with the low pressure provided the focus for isolated thunderstorms that produced two tornadoes over southeast Louisiana. In addition, persistent strong southeast and winds generated by a strong pressure gradient produced several reports of minor wind damage primarily to trees. Strong onshore wind caused tides to be 1 to 3 feet above normal, but locally higher.  Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred in low lying terrain in coastal areas and near tidal lakes. Very heavy rain also occurred with this system with several locations receiving as much as ten inches of rain over a 48 hour period. However, due to drought conditions prior to this storm, flooding was much less than usual.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado crossed Bayou Dularge and Louisiana Highway 315 about 8.9 miles southwest of the community of Theriot. Minor roof damage occurred to a few homes and sheet metal roof panels were pulled from roofs of storage buildings. Four medium sized boats were overturned in Bayou Dularge. Maximum winds were estimated at 75 mph. Damage path was approximately 150 yards long and 50 yards wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system moved along the coast of Louisiana water levels rose above 3 feet above MLLW. Minor flooding was reported from Sabine Pass to Cypremort and Burns Points. As the low pressure departed the area north winds pushed water levels below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds in the wake of a strong low pressure system and cold front pushed water levels below normal along the Cameron Parish coast. The tide at Cameron briefly touched 1 foot below MLLW at 7:09 AM CST.
